docs.title: La vita e le radici culturali
docs.text:
Arthur Schopenhauer nacque il 22 febbraio 1788 a Danzica, nell'odierna Polonia. Suo padre era un ricco banchiere, sua madre Giovanna una nota scrittrice di romanzi. Dopo la morte del padre abbandonò la carriera commerciale e si dedicò alla filosofia: studiò a Gottinga e a Berlino, si laureò a Jena nel 1813 e si trasferì a Dresda, dove tra il 1814 e il 1818 scrisse la sua opera principale, Il mondo come volontà e rappresentazione, pubblicata nel 1818. Il libro passò quasi inosservato. Nel 1820 ottenne la libera docenza a Berlino, ma le sue lezioni — fissate provocatoriamente negli stessi orari di quelle di Hegel — rimasero deserte. Dal 1831 visse a Francoforte sul Meno fino alla morte (1860). Il successo arrivò tardi: solo dopo il 1848, quando il fallimento dei moti rivoluzionari diffuse in Europa un'ondata di disillusione, il suo pessimismo trovò finalmente un pubblico pronto ad ascoltarlo.

La sua opera resta uno dei testi fondamentali della filosofia moderna, capace di influenzare profondamente non solo il pensiero successivo ma anche la letteratura, la musica e la psicologia. Il pensiero di Schopenhauer si pone come punto di incontro tra esperienze filosofiche molto diverse. Di Platone lo attrae la teoria delle idee come forme eterne; da Kant deriva l'impostazione soggettivistica della conoscenza, la convinzione cioè che noi non cogliamo la realtà così com'è ma così come la nostra mente la organizza; dell'Illuminismo lo interessa lo spirito ironico e demistificatore; dal Romanticismo trae l'irrazionalismo, l'importanza dell'arte e il tema del dolore — con la differenza che il Romanticismo tendeva a riscattare il negativo attraverso il positivo (Dio, lo Spirito, il progresso), mentre Schopenhauer approda a una visione decisamente pessimistica. Un posto del tutto particolare nella formazione di Schopenhauer spetta alla sapienza orientale: fu il primo grande filosofo occidentale a recuperare sistematicamente il pensiero indiano, in particolare le Upanishad e il buddhismo, da cui ricavò il concetto di Maya (illusione), la centralità della sofferenza e l'ideale dell'ascesi.

Un ruolo decisivo, anche se per contrasto, è quello di Hegel, bersaglio della sua polemica più feroce: lo definisce un "ciarlatano di mente ottusa", un "sicario della verità", accusandolo di aver ridotto la filosofia a strumento del potere. La polemica non era solo personale: Schopenhauer rifiutava alla radice l'idea che la realtà fosse governata da una Ragione e che la storia avesse un senso, un fine, un progresso. Per Hegel l'essenza della realtà è l'Idea, un'unica Ragione che si realizza e conosce se stessa; per Schopenhauer è la Volontà di vivere, una forza cieca e irrazionale che anima il Tutto senza alcuno scopo.

docs.title: Il velo di Maya
docs.text:
L'opera principale di Schopenhauer, Il mondo come volontà e rappresentazione, si articola in due grandi momenti: il mondo come rappresentazione e il mondo come volontà. Il punto di partenza è la distinzione kantiana tra fenomeno e noumeno, ma reinterpretata in modo radicale. Per Kant il fenomeno era l'unica realtà accessibile alla mente e il noumeno un concetto-limite inconoscibile. Per Schopenhauer il fenomeno è parvenza, illusione e sogno — ciò che nell'antica sapienza indiana si chiama il "velo di Maya" — mentre il noumeno è la realtà autentica che si nasconde dietro l'ingannevole trama delle apparenze e che il filosofo ha il compito di scoprire.

La rappresentazione, tuttavia, non è il nulla: è il mondo così come ci appare, regolato da leggi precise. Come Kant, Schopenhauer ritiene che la nostra mente organizzi l'esperienza attraverso forme a priori, ma ne ammette solo tre: spazio, tempo e causalità. Quest'ultima è l'unica vera categoria e assume forme diverse a seconda degli ambiti in cui opera: come necessità fisica regola i rapporti tra oggetti (principio del divenire), come necessità logica i rapporti tra premesse e conseguenze (principio del conoscere), come necessità matematica i rapporti spazio-temporali (principio dell'essere), come necessità morale le connessioni tra azioni e motivi (principio dell'agire). Poichè le forme a priori funzionano come vetri sfaccettati che deformano la visione, la rappresentazione è una fantasmagoria ingannevole e "la vita è sogno". Il mondo che percepiamo è "la mia rappresentazione": ha due aspetti inseparabili come le facce di una medaglia — il soggetto rappresentante e l'oggetto rappresentato — nessuno dei quali può esistere senza l'altro. Per questo sia il materialismo (che nega il soggetto) sia l'idealismo di Fichte (che nega l'oggetto) sono falsi.

docs.title: Tutto è volontà
docs.text:
Se il mondo fenomenico non è che un sogno, la domanda che si pone è: c'è qualcosa al di là del sogno? E se c'è, come possiamo raggiungerlo? La risposta di Schopenhauer è tanto semplice quanto geniale: il corpo. Se fossimo soltanto conoscenza, una "testa d'angelo alata senza corpo", non potremmo mai uscire dal mondo fenomenico. Ma poichè siamo anche corpo, non ci limitiamo a vederci dal di fuori: ci viviamo dal di dentro, godendo e soffrendo. Ripiegandoci su noi stessi, scopriamo che l'essenza profonda del nostro essere è la brama, la volontà di vivere (Wille zum Leben): un impulso irresistibile che ci spinge a esistere e ad agire. Il nostro corpo non è che la manifestazione esteriore delle nostre brame interiori — l'apparato digerente è l'aspetto fenomenico della volontà di nutrirsi, l'apparato sessuale della volontà di riprodursi — e l'intero mondo fenomenico non è altro che il modo in cui la volontà si rende visibile a se stessa. Il rapporto tra volontà e intelletto è quello tra padrone e servo, tra cavaliere e cavallo, tra il sole e la luna.

Questa scoperta rappresenta il cuore della filosofia di Schopenhauer: la volontà di vivere non è un concetto astratto, ma qualcosa che ciascuno può sperimentare direttamente in se stesso, nei propri desideri, nelle proprie paure, nella propria fame. Per analogia, Schopenhauer estende questa intuizione all'intero universo: la volontà di vivere non è solo l'essenza dell'uomo ma la cosa in sè di tutte le cose. Tutti gli esseri ne sono pervasi, in forme e gradi di consapevolezza diversi, dalla materia inorganica fino all'uomo. La volontà è inconscia (non è "volontà cosciente" ma energia, impulso cieco), unica (al di là di spazio e tempo non è soggetta al principio di individuazione: "è in una quercia come in un milione di querce"), eterna e indistruttibile, incausata e senza scopo. L'unico assoluto è la volontà stessa. Nel mondo fenomenico essa si oggettiva prima in un sistema di forme immutabili che Schopenhauer chiama platonicamente "idee" (archetipi eterni), poi nei singoli individui del mondo naturale, disposti in una "piramide cosmica" che dalle forze della natura sale fino all'uomo, in cui la volontà diviene pienamente consapevole — ma ciò che guadagna in coscienza, lo perde in sicurezza: la ragione è meno efficace dell'istinto, e per questo l'uomo è un "animale malaticcio".

docs.title: Il pessimismo
docs.text:
Dalla metafisica della volontà discende direttamente il pessimismo, che è la parte più celebre e influente della filosofia di Schopenhauer. Affermare che l'essere è volontà infinita equivale a dire che la vita è dolore per essenza. Volere significa desiderare, e desiderare significa trovarsi in uno stato di mancanza: il desiderio è assenza, vuoto, indigenza, ossia dolore. Per un desiderio appagato ne rimangono dieci insoddisfatti, e il desiderio soddisfatto dà immediatamente luogo a un desiderio nuovo, in una catena senza fine. La felicità non è dunque un possesso stabile, ma un'illusione che si dissolve nel momento stesso in cui sembra raggiungerci. Ciò che gli uomini chiamano "piacere" non è altro che la cessazione momentanea di un dolore — il godimento del bere presuppone la sofferenza della sete — mentre il dolore può esistere anche senza piacere che lo preceda: è il dato primario e permanente. Accanto al dolore e al piacere effimero, Schopenhauer pone la noia, che subentra quando viene meno il pungolo del desiderio. La vita umana è "un pendolo che oscilla incessantemente tra il dolore e la noia", passando attraverso l'intervallo fugace del piacere. "Non v'è rosa senza spine, ma vi sono parecchie spine senza rose."

Il dolore non riguarda solo l'uomo ma investe ogni creatura: dietro le "meraviglie" della natura si cela un'"arena di esseri tormentati che esistono solo a patto di divorarsi l'un l'altro". L'uomo soffre di più perchè ha maggiore consapevolezza, e il genio più di tutti: "più intelligenza avrai, più soffrirai". Schopenhauer perviene così a un pessimismo cosmico radicale: il male non è solo nel mondo, ma nel principio stesso da cui il mondo dipende. Se il dolore è universale, neppure l'amore offre una via di scampo: anch'esso è un inganno. Dietro le sue lusinghe si nasconde il "Genio della specie", che usa il piacere sessuale come trucco per indurre gli individui a perpetuare la vita e con essa il dolore. L'amore non è che "due infelicità che si incontrano, due infelicità che si scambiano e una terza infelicità che si prepara". Per questo l'unico amore degno di elogio non è quello dell'eros ma quello disinteressato della pietà.

Oltre a stabilire che il dolore è l'essenza della vita, Schopenhauer demolisce anche tutte le "menzogne" con cui gli uomini cercano di mascherare questa realtà, e per questo è considerato tra i "maestri del sospetto" accanto a Marx, Nietzsche e Freud, cioè tra quei pensatori che hanno insegnato a diffidare delle apparenze e a cercare dietro le credenze e i valori accettati dalla società le forze nascoste che li producono. Rifiuta l'ottimismo cosmico di chi vede il mondo come un organismo perfetto governato da Dio o dalla Ragione: il mondo è il teatro dell'illogicità e della sopraffazione, e se si conducesse il più ostinato ottimista attraverso gli ospedali, le prigioni e i campi di battaglia, finirebbe col rinunciare alle proprie illusioni. Rifiuta l'ottimismo sociale: i rapporti umani sono regolati dal conflitto e dalla sopraffazione, e gli uomini vivono insieme per bisogno, non per simpatia — lo Stato non è che un freno all'egoismo reciproco, una museruola che impedisce agli uomini di divorarsi a vicenda. Rifiuta l'ottimismo storico: la storia non è progresso ma "il fatale ripetersi di un medesimo dramma", sempre la stessa "monotona sonata", perchè il destino dell'uomo — nascita, sofferenza, morte — resta immutabile al di là del miraggio del tempo.

docs.title: Le vie della liberazione dal dolore
docs.text:
Di fronte a un quadro così desolante, si potrebbe pensare che questa filosofia conduca al suicidio, ma Schopenhauer lo condanna: il suicida non nega la volontà di vivere, anzi la afferma con forza, perchè "vuole la vita ed è solo malcontento delle condizioni che gli sono toccate". Inoltre il suicidio sopprime solo una manifestazione fenomenica della volontà, che rinasce in mille altri individui come il sole che risorge dall'altro lato. La vera liberazione non consiste nell'eliminare una vita, ma nel liberarsi dalla volontà di vivere stessa — nel passaggio dalla voluntas alla noluntas. Questo cammino si articola in tre tappe progressive, ciascuna delle quali rappresenta un grado crescente di liberazione.

La prima tappa è l'arte. L'arte è conoscenza libera e disinteressata che si rivolge alle idee eterne, sottraendo l'individuo alla catena dei bisogni e dei desideri. Chi contempla l'arte non è più l'individuo sottoposto alla volontà, ma il "puro occhio del mondo". Le arti sono ordinate gerarchicamente, dall'architettura alla tragedia; un posto a sè occupa la musica, che non riproduce le idee ma è "immediata rivelazione della volontà a se stessa", una "metafisica in suoni". Schopenhauer attribuisce alla musica il vertice assoluto fra le arti, perchè essa non imita il mondo fenomenico come la pittura o la scultura, ma coglie direttamente l'essenza della volontà, esprimendone i moti interiori — l'agitazione, il desiderio, la quiete, l'esaltazione — in un linguaggio universale che tutti gli uomini possono comprendere senza bisogno di parole. Per questo l'esperienza estetica produce quel particolare stato di serenità che si prova davanti a un bel paesaggio, ascoltando una sinfonia o leggendo una grande poesia: per un istante si cessa di volere e si contempla. Tuttavia la funzione liberatrice dell'arte è temporanea e parziale, un "breve incantesimo": terminata la contemplazione estetica, l'individuo ricade nella morsa della volontà e torna ad essere preda dei propri bisogni.

La seconda tappa della liberazione è la morale, che rappresenta un impegno nel mondo a favore del prossimo. Per Schopenhauer l'etica non nasce dalla ragione (come in Kant) ma da un sentimento di pietà, di "com-passione", attraverso cui avvertiamo come nostre le sofferenze degli altri. La formula delle Upanishad — Tat Twam Asi, "questo vivente sei tu" — esprime questa unità metafisica di tutti gli esseri: al di là del velo di Maya, al di là dell'illusione dell'individuazione, tutti gli esseri viventi sono la medesima volontà. La morale si concretizza nella giustizia (non fare il male) e nella carità (fare attivamente il bene), e ai suoi massimi livelli consiste nell'assumere su di sè il dolore cosmico. Questo tema richiama la "social catena" della Ginestra di Leopardi: entrambi vedono nella solidarietà tra esseri sofferenti l'unica risposta dignitosa al dolore. Tuttavia anche la morale resta dentro la vita: la pietà allevia la sofferenza ma non la elimina, perchè chi compatisce continua comunque a vivere e a volere. L'unica liberazione totale e definitiva è l'ascesi. Mentre l'arte sospende la volontà per un istante e la morale ne attenua gli effetti attraverso la solidarietà, l'ascesi va alla radice del problema: l'individuo, inorridito dalla volontà di vivere e dalla sofferenza universale che essa produce, si propone di estirpare il proprio desiderio di esistere, di godere e di volere. Le pratiche ascetiche sono molteplici e tutte convergono verso il medesimo fine: la castità perfetta spezza il ciclo della riproduzione, impedendo alla volontà di perpetuarsi in nuove creature destinate a soffrire; la rinuncia, il digiuno, la povertà e l'automacerazione tendono a sciogliere la volontà dalle proprie catene, privandola progressivamente di tutti gli oggetti a cui si aggrappa. Quando la coscienza del dolore diviene non un "motivo" ma un "quietivo" del volere, l'uomo diviene autenticamente libero. Il punto d'arrivo non è il Dio dei mistici cristiani ma il nirvana buddista: non il niente, ma un nulla relativo al mondo, un oceano di pace in cui le nozioni stesse di "io" e di "soggetto" si dissolvono, e con esse il dolore che accompagna ogni forma di esistenza individuale. A questo proposito Schopenhauer richiama le figure dei santi cristiani e degli asceti indiani, accomunati dal medesimo gesto: la rinuncia radicale al mondo. San Francesco d'Assisi, con la sua povertà volontaria, e il Buddha, con la sua meditazione sotto l'albero della Bodhi, sono per Schopenhauer incarnazioni diverse del medesimo ideale: la negazione della volontà, la scelta consapevole di non volere più.

Va detto che molti critici considerano questa teoria la parte più debole del sistema: se la volontà è l'assoluto, come può l'asceta annullarla? Schopenhauer sembra introdurre una contraddizione nel cuore stesso della propria metafisica, ammettendo che ciò che ha dichiarato infinito e incausato possa tuttavia essere vinto da un atto della coscienza individuale. Queste obiezioni tuttavia non cancellano nè la portata della denuncia del dolore, nè la profondità delle analisi schopenhaueriane sulla condizione umana. Con la sua filosofia, Schopenhauer ha aperto la strada a una serie di riflessioni decisive per il pensiero contemporaneo: l'idea che dietro la coscienza razionale si nascondano forze oscure e irrazionali anticipa la teoria dell'inconscio di Freud; la critica radicale all'ottimismo e al progresso prepara il terreno a Nietzsche; l'attenzione alla sofferenza e all'assurdo della condizione umana confluirà nell'esistenzialismo del Novecento. L'influsso di Schopenhauer si è esteso ben oltre la filosofia, raggiungendo scrittori come Thomas Mann e Marcel Proust, e musicisti come Richard Wagner, che considerò Il mondo come volontà e rappresentazione il libro più importante della sua vita.

docs.title: Schema riassuntivo
docs.text:
flowchart TD
    A["Il mondo come rappresentazione"] --> B["Fenomeno = velo di Maya"]
    B --> C["Forme a priori: spazio, tempo, causalità"]
    A --> D["Il mondo come volontà"]
    D --> E["Il corpo: via d'accesso al noumeno"]
    E --> F["Volontà di vivere: unica, cieca, eterna, incausata"]
    F --> G["Il pessimismo"]
    G --> H["Dolore, piacere effimero, noia"]
    G --> I["Sofferenza universale e pessimismo cosmico"]
    G --> J["Critica a ogni forma di ottimismo"]
    F --> K["Le vie della liberazione"]
    K --> L["Arte: sollievo temporaneo"]
    K --> M["Morale: compassione, giustizia, carità"]
    K --> N["Ascesi: dalla voluntas alla noluntas = nirvana"]

docs.title: Checklist
docs.text:
• Biografia e contesto storico
  • Le radici culturali: Platone, Kant, Illuminismo, Romanticismo, sapienza orientale
  • L'opposizione a Hegel
  • Il mondo come rappresentazione: velo di Maya, forme a priori, soggetto e oggetto
  • Il mondo come volontà: il corpo come via d'accesso, la volontà di vivere
  • I caratteri della volontà e i gradi di oggettivazione
  • Il pessimismo: dolore, piacere, noia, sofferenza universale
  • L'illusione dell'amore
  • La critica all'ottimismo cosmico, sociale e storico
  • Le vie della liberazione: arte, morale, ascesi
  • Dalla voluntas alla noluntas: il nirvana

docs.title: Collegamenti
docs.text:
• Italiano: Giacomo Leopardi e il pessimismo cosmico, la "social catena" della Ginestra come risposta solidale al dolore universale, il tema del piacere come cessazione del dolore nel Dialogo della Natura e di un Islandese
  • Latino: Lucrezio e la visione della natura come forza indifferente all'uomo nel De rerum natura; Seneca e il tema del dolore e della virtù stoica come distacco dalle passioni
  • Storia: il fallimento dei moti del 1848 e l'ondata di pessimismo in Europa che favorì la fortuna tardiva di Schopenhauer
  • Scienze: Darwin e la lotta per la sopravvivenza come "legge della giungla", la natura come arena di conflitto e non come disegno provvidenziale
  • Arte: il Romanticismo e il tema del dolore, dell'infinito, del sublime; la musica come arte suprema richiama il ruolo centrale della musica nel Romanticismo (Beethoven, Wagner)
  • Filosofia: Kant e il noumeno; Hegel come anti-modello; anticipazione di Nietzsche (ateismo, critica alla morale), di Freud (l'inconscio, la sessualità come forza dominante) e dell'esistenzialismo (l'angoscia, l'assurdo)
  • Inglese: Thomas Hardy e la visione pessimistica dell'esistenza nella letteratura vittoriana; Samuel Beckett e il teatro dell'assurdo come espressione della noia e dell'insensatezza della vita

docs.title: Contenuti
docs.text: Pagina introduttiva all'epoca "Global Age" e alle principali tematiche della letteratura contemporanea.

docs.title: Checklist
docs.text:
• Teoria
  • Esempi
  • Esercizi svolti

docs.title: Collegamenti
docs.text: Sezione per i collegamenti interdisciplinari utili per l'orale.

docs.title: A New World
docs.text: After the Victorian Age, English society changed very quickly. In the 19th century religious faith had been questioned; in the early 20th century people found it difficult to believe in anything at all — religion, philosophy, art, science or progress. Three great changes shook the way people thought about reality. In 1905 Albert Einstein published the Theory of Relativity, and time and space were no longer absolute: reality could no longer be described as objective. Friedrich Nietzsche declared that traditional values, such as "the good", "the true" and "the beautiful", were decadent, and his ideas deeply influenced British writers. Sigmund Freud developed psychoanalysis and showed that the human mind has many hidden layers, the unconscious, where universal patterns guide behaviour. As a result, modernist writers no longer trusted the outside world: they turned inwards, towards the mind and the flow of thought.

docs.title: What is Modernism?
docs.text:
The term Modernism describes the literature and art of the early 20th century that reacted against 19th-century conventions in completely new forms. Modernist writers broke down traditional literary genres, fragmented time and place, abandoned the linear plot, and used a complex language that often defied syntax, grammar and punctuation. They focused on psychological truth rather than realistic details, freely incorporated ancient and oriental myths, and preferred free verse to traditional metres. Although they were influenced by avant-garde movements like Cubism, Futurism, Dadaism and Surrealism, they did not reject the past: on the contrary, they tried to rescue what was valuable from tradition and use it to read the modern world.

Critics usually divide Modernists into two generations. The first worked between 1922 and 1925, the years of the great masterpieces — Eliot's The Waste Land, Joyce's Ulysses and Woolf's Mrs Dalloway — and explored the unconscious in daily life. The second generation came in the 1930s, led by W.H. Auden, and dealt with social and political problems in a less obscure language. Some of them, like George Orwell, even went to fight in the Spanish Civil War against the fascist forces of General Franco.

docs.title: Modern Poetry
docs.text: Between 1900 and 1918 English poetry changed completely, especially thanks to two movements started by Ezra Pound: Imagism, which fixed clear thoughts in clear images, and Vorticism, which celebrated energy and dynamism. In Ireland, W.B. Yeats led the Celtic Revival and wrote Easter 1916, a poem that commemorates the Irish revolt against British rule. The central modernist poem, however, is The Waste Land (1922) by T.S. Eliot, built on the myth of the Holy Grail and on the idea that Western civilisation has become sterile and chaotic. Its style is typically modernist: no clear narrative order, juxtaposition of past and present, time shifts, stream of consciousness and many quotations in different languages. In the 1930s Auden's Refugee Blues dealt with the Jewish people fleeing Nazi Germany, and in the 1940s the New Apocalypse poets, inspired by the Welsh poet Dylan Thomas, returned to nature and emotion, becoming "new Romantics".

docs.title: Modern Novel
docs.text: The English novel changed in two stages. Between 1880 and 1920 the transitional novelists experimented with flashbacks, time-shifts and new ways of describing psychology. Joseph Conrad, in Heart of Darkness (1902), used an exotic setting to explore the dark side of man and to criticise European colonialism. D.H. Lawrence was the first to feel the impact of psychoanalysis: his Sons and Lovers (1913) describes an Oedipal complex against the background of provincial life. In the late 1910s and early 1920s the modern novel proper emerged with Joyce and Woolf, who reflected the loss of faith in tradition and the trauma of World War I. The greatest change was the breakdown of the division of time: past, present and future became a continuous flux, and the technical solution was the stream of consciousness, also called interior monologue. Later, E.M. Forster explored colonial themes in A Passage to India, and George Orwell created the dystopian novel with Nineteen Eighty-Four (1949), where Big Brother became the symbol of modern man's enslavement to mass media.

docs.title: Joyce and Eliot
docs.text: Joyce and Eliot are the two greatest modernists in English literature, and they share more than they seem. Both used the mythical method: Joyce based Ulysses on Homer's Odyssey, while Eliot built The Waste Land on the legend of the Holy Grail. Both fragmented their texts, juxtaposing past and present and using the stream of consciousness or a similar collage of voices to show the chaos of modern life. Both also focused on a great city, Dublin for Joyce and London for Eliot, presented as places of paralysis and sterility. The differences are equally important. Joyce wrote prose and chose an ordinary man, Leopold Bloom, as his hero, celebrating with affectionate irony the small details of everyday life. Eliot wrote poetry and described modern civilisation as a dying wasteland, with a more pessimistic tone and a constant search for spiritual meaning that would later lead him to Christianity. Where Joyce remains rooted in the body, in sex and in the comic, Eliot is intellectual, religious and tragic.

docs.title: Joyce and Woolf
docs.text: Joyce and Woolf are the two great novelists of Modernism, and both used the stream of consciousness to describe how a single day can contain a whole life — Bloomsday in Ulysses, Clarissa's day in Mrs Dalloway. Both rejected the omniscient narrator and placed the reader directly inside the mind of their characters. They differ, however, in style and atmosphere. Joyce's stream of consciousness is rough, ironic, almost chaotic, and reaches its extreme in Molly Bloom's monologue without punctuation. Woolf's stream is softer and more poetic: in her essay Modern Fiction she explained that what matters is not "time on the clock" but "time in the mind". Joyce uses the mythical method openly, while Woolf prefers psychological subtlety and is especially interested in female consciousness, as in To the Lighthouse.

docs.title: Woolf and Eliot
docs.text: Woolf and Eliot belonged to the same London circle and both explored inner time and the crisis of values. Eliot, however, expressed this crisis through poetry, ancient myths and images of sterility, while Woolf used the novel to follow the delicate flow of personal consciousness, often from a female point of view.

docs.title: Checklist
docs.text:
• Cambiamento di ideali a inizio Novecento (Einstein, Nietzsche, Freud)
  • Definizione e caratteristiche del Modernismo
  • Prima e seconda generazione
  • Cambiamenti nella poesia e nel romanzo
  • Confronti Joyce/Eliot, Joyce/Woolf, Woolf/Eliot

docs.title: Collegamenti
docs.text:
• Italiano: la crisi dei valori richiama Pirandello, Svevo e Montale; il "tempo interiore" si collega alla coscienza di Zeno
  • Storia: Prima guerra mondiale, crisi del 1929, fascismo e nazismo, Guerra civile spagnola
  • Filosofia: Nietzsche e la crisi dei valori, Bergson e il tempo interiore, Freud e l'inconscio
  • Fisica: Einstein e la relatività, la fine del tempo e dello spazio assoluti
  • Arte: Cubismo, Futurismo, Dadaismo, Surrealismo — la frammentazione del reale
  • Latino: il riuso del mito (Ulisse in Joyce) come Dante nel canto XXVI dell'Inferno

docs.title: Introduction
docs.text: "The Fire Sermon" is the third section of The Waste Land. Eliot took the title from a famous sermon by Buddha, in which Buddha teaches that man must free himself from earthly things — desire, passion, possession — because only ascetism can bring true happiness. The title also recalls Baudelaire and his Flowers of Evil, an oxymoron that was very important for Eliot. In this section Eliot doesn't describe the modern world in an objective way: he uses symbols and short scenes to show different love stories of his time. His aim is to make us think about love, and to show how modern love has been reduced to simple lust — love, the purest of all feelings, becomes only sex, which produces no children. For this reason the whole section is full of images of sterility. Eliot shows three kinds of love: Queen Elizabeth with the Count of Leicester, the Smyrna merchant, and the typist with a young clerk.

docs.title: The Smyrna merchant
docs.text: The first scene shows Mr. Eugenides, the Smyrna merchant: an untidy man, unshaven, with a pocket full of currants. He asks the narrator, in a bad French, to have lunch with him at the Cannon Street Hotel and to spend a weekend at the Metropole, a hotel known as a place for homosexuals. The currants are a symbol of sterility, so Eliot is speaking of a homosexual love that cannot give life.

docs.title: Tiresias
docs.text: The next scene is introduced by Tiresias, the blind old prophet of Greek mythology. The myth says that one day he saw two snakes mating and separated them with a stick: for this he became a woman. Years later he saw them again, killed the male snake, and became a man again. Hera, the wife of Zeus, made him blind because Tiresias had said that women feel more pleasure than men. To repair this, Zeus gave him the gift of seeing beyond appearances. This is why in the poem Tiresias is a blind old man with wrinkled female breasts — now he is a man, but he was a woman before.

docs.title: The typist and the clerk
docs.text: Tiresias watches a typist coming home from work at sunset. The food in tins, the clothes piled on the only divan where she sleeps at night, the stockings, camisoles and stays thrown around, all suggest dryness, old age and a careless life. Then her lover arrives: a small house agent's clerk with a bold stare — a vulgar young man who has recently become rich, without any class. The meal is over, she is bored and tired, and he tries to convince her with caresses, then he attacks her. She doesn't refuse, but she doesn't want him either: her indifference becomes, for him, a kind of welcome. Tiresias, who has already suffered everything and has sat by the walls of Thebes among the dead, has seen this scene many times before on the same divan or bed. The man leaves with a final patronising kiss and finds his way down the dark stairs. She turns and looks at herself for a moment in the mirror, barely aware that her lover has gone. Only one half-formed thought passes through her mind: "Well now that's done, and I'm glad it's over." Then she walks around the room alone, tidies her hair with an automatic hand, and puts a record on the gramophone.

docs.title: The meaning of the section
docs.text: The title doesn't seem connected to the content of these scenes, but this is typical of Eliot's mythical method: he uses ancient myth not to contrast it with the present, but to put past and present next to each other, because only through the past can we understand our own time (the same idea that Joyce uses with Molly and Penelope). The two couples Eliot describes are an objective correlative — concrete images and situations that create in the reader the same feeling of sterility and emptiness that the poet wants to communicate, without saying it directly. This is also why Eliot is one of the greatest authors of Modernism.

docs.title: Checklist
docs.text:
• Titolo e riferimento al sermone di Buddha e a Baudelaire
  • Tema centrale: amore ridotto a lussuria, sterilità
  • I tre tipi di amore presentati
  • Mr. Eugenides, il mercante di Smirne
  • Il mito di Tiresia
  • La tipista e il giovane "clerk"
  • Metodo mitico e correlativo oggettivo

docs.title: Collegamenti
docs.text:
• Italiano: Montale e la "poetica dell'oggetto" (Ossi di seppia) — il correlativo oggettivo arriva in Italia proprio da Eliot; la sterilità e l'aridità del paesaggio ligure richiamano la "waste land"
  • Filosofia: Schopenhauer e la vita come dolore, il buddhismo e l'ascesi come liberazione dal desiderio
  • Storia: la crisi dei valori dopo la Prima guerra mondiale, la società di massa e la perdita di senso
  • Latino/Greco: il mito di Tiresia (Ovidio, Metamorfosi, libro III), la Divina Commedia di Dante (Tiresia è tra gli indovini dell'Inferno, canto XX)
  • Arte: la tecnica del collage nelle avanguardie (Cubismo, Dadaismo) — immagini accostate senza nesso logico

docs.title: Life and Works
docs.text:
James Joyce is one of the great writers of Modernism, together with T.S. Eliot, Virginia Woolf and Ezra Pound. Unlike the others, Joyce was Irish — he came from a country that was still fighting for its independence from Britain, and this shaped all of his work.

He was born in Dublin in 1882 into a middle-class Catholic family whose social status and financial resources gradually declined during his childhood. His father, John Stanislaus, had supported Charles Parnell, the leader of the Home Rule movement, but after Parnell's death in 1891 he retired from politics and social life. Joyce later described him in Portrait of the Artist as a Young Man (1916) as "a praiser of his own past." From the age of six, Joyce attended two Jesuit schools, whose strict intellectual training deeply influenced his writing. In 1898 he entered University College, Dublin, to study modern languages.

Even though he grew up in Irish culture, Joyce was a rebel from a young age. He rejected the Catholic faith and refused to take part in the Celtic Revival or other forms of Irish artistic life. He was strongly influenced by the Norwegian writer Henrik Ibsen, whose portraits of individuals trapped by social norms reflected Joyce's own experience of provincial Ireland. Joyce's relationship with his country was complex: he rejected Irish public life and nationalism, yet all of his works are set in Dublin. Living abroad gave him the distance he needed to write about his city honestly.

In 1904 Joyce met Nora Barnacle, a woman from Galway, on 16 June — the same day on which the story of Ulysses takes place. They left Ireland together and lived in Trieste, Paris and Zurich. Joyce chose a life of exile, but he never stopped writing about Dublin.

His career was marked by a troubled publication history. Dubliners was rejected 18 times by 15 publishers before being published in 1914, because publishers considered it "immoral" for its honest portrait of Irish life. Ulysses was banned in Britain and America from 1922 until 1933. His last novel, Finnegans Wake, took him sixteen years to finish.

After Dubliners came out, Ezra Pound and T.S. Eliot helped Joyce find financial support. By the 1920s he was seen as one of the prophets of Modernism. In Trieste he became the centre of a literary circle and grew close to Italo Svevo, the Italian writer, whom he introduced to psychoanalysis. Joyce also wrote poetry: Chamber Music (1907) and the moving poem Ecce Puer (1932), written when his grandson was born, shortly after his own father died.

Joyce's last years were not easy. His daughter Lucia had a mental breakdown in 1932, and Joyce himself suffered from serious eye problems, going through many operations against increasing blindness. In 1940, when France was occupied by the Nazis, he moved to Zurich, where he died in January 1941.

All of Joyce's works are connected: they share the same setting — Dublin — and the same characters appear in different books. His writing moved from the particular to the universal, from the short stories of Dubliners to the epic scale of Ulysses, which is based on Homer's Odyssey.

docs.title: Dubliners (1914)
docs.text:
Dubliners is a collection of fifteen short stories. In each of them, the failure of self-realisation of an inhabitant of Dublin is examined in biographical and psychological detail. As Joyce wrote, "My intention was to write a chapter of the moral history of my country and I chose Dublin for the scene because that city seemed to me the centre of paralysis."

"Paralysis" — the inability to act, to find courage, to know oneself — is the central theme of the whole book. It leads ordinary people to accept the limitations imposed by the social context they live in. Joyce organised the stories in four stages: childhood, youth, maturity and public life. The last story, "The Dead," brings them all together. The point of view also changes: the first three stories are told in the first person, the rest in the third person.

Dubliners is not fully Modernist — it uses both Realism and Symbolism. On one hand, it describes characters, streets, pubs and ways of speaking in Dublin with great accuracy. On the other hand, Joyce uses a symbolic technique: ordinary objects suddenly gain a deeper meaning through what he called an "epiphany" — a moment when something simple makes a person realise an uncomfortable truth about themselves. These epiphanies are never happy discoveries; they reveal comfortable, accepted truths as fallacies.

Joyce's style is also different from traditional short stories. Writers like Poe or Henry James used surprises and plot twists. Joyce, like the Russian writer Chekhov, followed the natural flow of everyday life: his stories begin in the middle of something and end without a clear conclusion. Nothing dramatic happens, yet behind the routine of ordinary lives, the pressing issues of contemporary Irish life are clearly present: Ireland's difficult relations with Britain, the tensions of family ties, and the strong presence of religion.

docs.title: Eveline
docs.text:
"Eveline" is the fourth story in Dubliners and belongs to the youth group. But in Dublin, youth does not mean energy or hope: the story opens at the end of the day, with Eveline sitting at her window, watching the world outside. She is tired — not in her body, but tired of the life she lives. Joyce implies her tiredness with the life she is living rather than actual physical fatigue.

Eveline has agreed to leave Dublin with Frank, a kind sailor who wants to take her to Buenos Aires. As she sits at the window, she looks around her room at the familiar objects she has dusted once a week for so many years, wondering where on earth all the dust comes from. She thinks she will never see those objects again, and yet she has never even found out the name of the priest whose yellowing photograph hangs on the wall.

Her life has been difficult. Her mother is dead, her brother Ernest is dead too, and the Waters have gone back to England. Her father is violent, drinks, and controls the family money — Eveline must hand over her entire wages and her brother Harry's too. She works hard to keep the house together, making sure the two younger children go to school and get their meals regularly. It is a hard life, and yet now that she is about to leave it, she does not find it a wholly undesirable life.

Then she thinks of Frank. He is very kind, manly, open-hearted. He has a home waiting for her in Buenos Aires. She remembers how they met, how he took her to see The Bohemian Girl at the theatre, how he sang about "the lass that loves a sailor." He had started as a deck boy and had sailed through the Straits of Magellan. Frank represents her chance to escape — but her father found out about the affair and forbade her to see him.

The turning point comes when she remembers the promise she made to her dying mother: to keep the family together as long as she could. She hears again her mother's last words, and a feeling of terror rises in her. She must escape! Frank would save her. He would give her life, perhaps love too.

At the dock, among the crowd, Frank holds her hand. The boat blows a long mournful whistle into the mist. But at the last moment, Eveline cannot move. All the seas of the world tumble about her heart. She grips the iron railing with both hands, her face white, like a helpless animal. Frank calls "Come!" — but her eyes show him no sign of love, farewell or recognition. She is paralysed.

Joyce shows that Eveline is trapped between two forces: her desire for a new life and her sense of duty. The promise to her mother acts like a chain, and the fear of the unknown becomes paralysis — the same paralysis that runs through every story in the collection.

docs.title: Checklist
docs.text:
• Joyce: life, works, themes, style
  • Dubliners: structure, paralysis, epiphany, style
  • Eveline: summary and analysis

docs.title: Collegamenti
docs.text:
• Italiano: la paralisi e l'incapacità di agire richiamano gli "inetti" di Italo Svevo (La coscienza di Zeno), che Joyce conobbe a Trieste e a cui presentò la psicoanalisi
  • Storia: l'Irlanda tra Ottocento e Novecento — Home Rule, Parnell, Easter Rising 1916, emigrazione
  • Filosofia: paralisi della volontà e libertà di scelta — collegamento con l'esistenzialismo e la "cattiva fede" di Sartre
  • Arte: il Realismo nella descrizione degli ambienti dublinesi
  • Scienze: Joyce e la psicoanalisi, il ruolo dell'inconscio nelle decisioni dei personaggi

docs.title: The Dead
docs.text:
"The Dead" is the last and longest story in Dubliners and the climax of the whole collection. Unlike the other stories, which focus on individual paralysis, "The Dead" brings together many characters and offers a more compassionate view of Dublin.

The story begins with a party that the elderly Morkan sisters organise each year at the Epiphany. They invite friends and relatives, both Catholics and Protestants, and the guests discuss Irish politics. The central character is Gabriel Conroy, nephew to the Morkan sisters. He is an intelligent journalist, but he has an unfulfilled personality: he would like to take sides on Ireland's problems, travel to Europe, and write freely, but he prefers not to expose himself and continues his usual life. Late at night, when the party is over, Gabriel and his wife Gretta go back to their hotel. Gabriel desires his wife, but Gretta is lost in thought.

docs.title: "I Think He Died for Me"
docs.text:
At the hotel, Gabriel kisses Gretta and his heart is full of happiness. He puts his hands on her hair and begins smoothing it back, scarcely touching it with his fingers. He is sure she feels the same desire he feels. But when he asks "Gretta dear, what are you thinking about?", she does not answer. He asks again, softly. Then she says in an outburst of tears: "O, I am thinking about that song, The Lass of Aughrim" — a song she heard at the party. She breaks free from him and runs to the bed, throwing her arms across the bed-rail to hide her face. Gabriel stands still for a moment in astonishment.

He follows her and asks why the song makes her cry. She raises her head and dries her eyes with the back of her hand, like a child. She tells him she is thinking about a person she used to know long ago, a young boy named Michael Furey. He used to sing that song. He was very delicate, with big dark eyes, and they used to go out walking together when she was in Galway. Gabriel asks if she was in love with him, and she says "I was great with him at that time."

Gabriel feels humiliated by the thought of this figure from the dead — a boy in the gasworks. While he had been full of memories of tenderness, joy and desire, she had been comparing him in her mind with another man. A dull anger begins to gather at the back of his mind. He sees himself as a ridiculous figure — "a pennyboy for his aunts, a nervous well-meaning sentimentalist, orating to vulgarians and idealising his own clownish lusts." He catches a glimpse of himself in the mirror and turns his back to the light, ashamed.

Gretta reveals that Michael Furey was ill with consumption (tuberculosis). He was only seventeen. When the time came for her to leave Galway and go to the convent in Dublin, he was very ill. She wrote him a letter saying she would be back in the summer. But the night before she left, she heard gravel thrown up against her window. She ran downstairs and there was the poor fellow, at the end of the garden, shivering in the rain. She told him to go home at once or he would get his death. But he said he did not want to live. She can see his eyes as well as she can see Gabriel's. A week later, at the convent, she heard that he was dead. "I think he died for me," she answered.

docs.title: "The Living and the Dead"
docs.text:
This is the concluding part. Gretta has cried herself to sleep. Gabriel, leaning on his elbow, looks at her for a few moments without resentment. So she had had that romance in her life: a man had died for her sake. It hardly pains him now to think how poor a part he, her husband, has played in her life. He watches her while she sleeps as though he and she had never lived together as man and wife. His curious eyes rest long upon her face and on her hair, and as he thinks of what she must have been then, in that time of her first girlish beauty, a strange friendly pity enters his soul. He does not like to say even to himself that her face is no longer beautiful, but he knows that it is no longer the face for which Michael Furey had braved death.

The air of the room chills his shoulders. He stretches himself cautiously along under the sheets and lies down beside his wife. One by one they were all becoming shades. He thinks: "Better pass boldly into that other world, in the full glory of some passion, than fade and wither dismally with age." He thinks of how the boy who lay beside Gretta in her heart for so many years had told her that he did not wish to live. Generous tears fill Gabriel's eyes. He has never felt like that himself towards any woman, but he knows that such a feeling must be love. The tears gather more thickly in his eyes and in the partial darkness he imagines a young man standing under a dripping tree. Other forms are near.

His soul has approached that region where the vast hosts of the dead dwell. He is conscious of, but cannot understand, their wayward and flickering existence. His own identity is fading out into a grey impalpable world. A few light taps upon the window pane make him turn to the window. It has begun to snow again. He watches sleepily the flakes, silver and dark, falling obliquely against the lamplight. The time has come for him to set out on his journey westward. Snow is falling on every part of the dark central plain, on the treeless hills, falling softly upon the Bog of Allen and, farther westward, softly falling into the dark mutinous Shannon waves. It is falling, too, upon every part of the lonely churchyard on the hill where Michael Furey lies buried. It lies thickly on the crooked crosses and headstones, on the spears of the little gate, on the barren thorns. His soul swoones slowly as he hears the snow falling faintly through the universe and faintly falling, like the descent of their last end, upon all the living and the dead.

The Epiphany in The Dead

Gabriel's epiphany is the most complex in Dubliners. The snow falling outside makes him realise he has never experienced true love — not the selfless passion that Michael Furey felt. The snow is the central symbol: it falls on the living and the dead, uniting them in a common destiny.

The Snow as Symbol

The snow represents death, purification, stillness, and above all a common destiny shared by all people. It erases the boundary between the living and the dead. It is one of the most famous endings in English literature.

docs.title: Ulysses (1922)
docs.text: Ulysses is a modernist novel set in Dublin on a single day — 16 June 1904. Its title refers to Homer's Odyssey, and its eighteen chapters are modelled on episodes from the ancient epic. Joyce completed the novel over seven years in Trieste, Zurich and Paris, and published it in Paris in 1922.

docs.title: The Story
docs.text:
The novel tells the story of a day in the life of three characters. Leopold Bloom is a Dubliner of Jewish origin who works as an advertising agent. He is the Ulysses of the title and wanders about Dublin just as Homer's Ulysses wandered about the Mediterranean. His day is quite banal: he gets up, goes to a Turkish bath, attends a funeral, goes to his newspaper office, has lunch, wanders about, goes to the library, meets people, goes to a pub, sits at a beach, visits a hospital, and ends up at a brothel where at midnight he meets Stephen Dedalus and takes him home.

Stephen Dedalus is the central character of the first part — a sensitive young man with literary ambitions who feels frustrated by Irish provincial life. He is looking for a father figure, which he finds in Bloom. Homeless and fatherless, he corresponds to Telemachus, the son of Ulysses. Molly Bloom, Leopold's wife, is the central character of the last part. She corresponds to Penelope, but unlike Penelope she is unfaithful. The novel ends with her famous stream-of-consciousness monologue.

docs.title: The Mythical Structure
docs.text:
Each chapter corresponds to an episode in the Odyssey: the modern Nestor is headmaster of a school; Bloom's trip to a funeral is a visit to the underworld; the Sirens are barmaids; Molly's thoughts at night reveal an unfaithful Penelope. Joyce uses the epic model to show the lack of heroism, ideals, love and trust in the modern world. Together with Eliot's The Waste Land, Ulysses is one of the greatest examples of the reworking of myth in modernist literature.

The Linati Schema

In 1920, Joyce sent a schema of the novel to his Italian friend Carlo Linati. Each chapter was given a title from the Odyssey (Calipso, Eolo, Sirene, Ciclope, Itaca, Penelope...) and Joyce gave indications for its interpretation: the time of day, the characters, the technique, the organ of the body, the colour, the symbol, and the art.

docs.title: The Stream of Consciousness
docs.text: Ulysses is famous for being written using the stream-of-consciousness technique — a prose style which removes syntactic connectives and puts together disparate images to show the chaotic flow of thoughts in the human mind. The term was coined by the American philosopher William James (1842–1910). Joyce's technique includes: short sentences without a main verb; events interrupted by unrelated thoughts; abrupt images from the past; questions not meant for anyone; and a musical quality based on assonance and alliteration.

docs.title: "Yes I Said Yes I Will Yes" — Molly's Monologue
docs.text:
The final episode, entitled "Penelope," is Molly Bloom's famous interior monologue. The stream-of-consciousness technique is taken to its extreme: there is no third-person narrator and no punctuation at all — no paragraphs, no commas, no full stops. Nothing suggests an order or a hierarchy of thoughts.

Molly is lying in bed, half thinking, half dreaming. Her thoughts move freely between the present and the past, mixing memories, fantasies and desires with no logical order. She thinks about the day Bloom proposed to her on Howth Head, a hill near Dublin. They were lying among the rhododendrons, and she gave him a bit of seedcake out of her mouth. She remembers her youth in Gibraltar — the Spanish girls laughing in their shawls, the old castle, the handsome Moors in white, the little shops with their old windows, the glorious sunsets and the sea "sometimes like fire." She thinks about other kisses and other men, mixing everything together in one continuous flow.

The passage ends with the famous words "yes I said yes I will Yes" — Molly's memory of the moment she accepted Bloom's proposal. The final "Yes" is one of the most famous words in all of literature: an affirmation of life, of love, of sensuality, of acceptance. It is important to notice that Molly says "I got him to propose to me," suggesting that she was the one who made it happen, not Bloom.

Why No Punctuation?

Joyce removes all punctuation to reproduce the continuous, chaotic flow of thoughts in a half-sleeping mind. The reader is placed directly inside Molly's consciousness, with no narrator to provide guidance.

docs.title: Censorship and Publication
docs.text: Ulysses had serious problems with censorship. Serialisation began in the American Little Review in 1918 but was stopped when a chapter was considered obscene. The Hogarth Press (owned by Leonard and Virginia Woolf) also turned it down. The novel was eventually published thanks to Sylvia Beach, owner of the Shakespeare & Co. Bookshop in Paris. Copies were confiscated at the US customs house. In the US it could not be distributed until 1933, in England until 1936, and in Ireland until the 1960s.

docs.title: Checklist
docs.text:
• The Dead: summary, "I Think He Died for Me," "The Living and the Dead"
  • Ulysses: structure, characters, mythical method, stream of consciousness
  • Molly's monologue: "Yes I Said Yes I Will Yes"

docs.title: Collegamenti
docs.text:
• Italiano: il monologo interiore di Molly Bloom si collega al flusso di coscienza in Svevo; il tema amore/morte in The Dead richiama il Decadentismo
  • Storia: la censura e il rapporto tra arte e morale nella società del primo Novecento
  • Filosofia: il tempo interiore di Bergson e il flusso di coscienza; la ricerca di identità in Gabriel Conroy richiama l'esistenzialismo
  • Arte: il Cubismo e la frammentazione della realtà in Ulysses
  • Scienze: William James e la psicologia sperimentale, la psicoanalisi e l'inconscio
  • Latino: il viaggio di Ulisse nell'Odissea e la sua rielaborazione in Joyce, come Dante nel canto XXVI dell'Inferno

docs.title: Life and Works
docs.text:
T.S. Eliot was the leading poet of Modernism in the 1910s and 1920s. In 1922 he published The Waste Land, a long poem with quotations in six languages and adaptations from Dante, Baudelaire and many other writers, following a collage technique typical of Modernism. Eliot was born in the US but lived most of his life in England. He was a great experimenter but also a classicist. He started with a deeply pessimistic view of life, which later turned into Christian acceptance. He received the Nobel Prize for literature in 1948.

Thomas Stearns Eliot was born in 1888 into a rich family of St Louis, Missouri. The Eliots descended from New England Puritans, and a strong sense of civic and religious duty was part of the family tradition. In 1906 he went to Harvard, where he studied Renaissance literature, philosophy, Hindu and Buddhist philosophy, and Sanskrit — all of which later entered The Waste Land. He graduated with a dissertation on the philosopher F.H. Bradley, whose ideas on the psychological nature of experience influenced his poetry. After Harvard he went to the Sorbonne in Paris (1910), where he studied the philosophy of Henri Bergson and began to read the French Symbolist poets. He then travelled to Germany and, at the beginning of World War I in 1914, moved to Merton College, Oxford.

1915 was a crucial year. Eliot moved to London and went to work at Lloyd's Bank, a job he held until 1925 but never liked. He also married Vivienne Haigh-Wood, a talented woman afflicted by bad health and bad nerves. He had begun to write but was not happy with the poetry of the time, which seemed imprecise and out of touch with the modern world. The meeting with Ezra Pound and the poetry of Imagism, with its insistence on clear and precise images, was decisive. Through Pound, the magazine Poetry published Eliot's first major poem, The Love Song of J. Alfred Prufrock (1915), followed by Poems (1919). These early poems deal with the emptiness of modern upper-class life and owe much to the French Symbolists.

docs.title: The Nihilistic Phase
docs.text: The years 1915–25 were very hard for Eliot. Money problems, working full-time in a bank, poor relations with his wife, and a general dissatisfaction with modern life brought him to a nervous breakdown in November 1921. He went to rest first in Margate (southern England) and then in a Swiss sanatorium in Lausanne. During this time he finished the first draft of The Waste Land. On his way back to London he stopped in Paris to give the manuscript to Ezra Pound, who made extensive cuts and helped shape the final version. The Waste Land came out in 1922, making Eliot the leading modernist poet in English. He left the bank and in 1925 became a director of the publishing house Faber and Gwyer (now Faber and Faber). The central metaphor of this nihilistic phase — which also includes Gerontion (1919) and The Hollow Men (1925) — is one of sterility, aridity, impotence.

docs.title: The Christian Phase and Late Years
docs.text:
In the late 1920s Eliot found a way out of nihilism in religion. He was received into the Anglican Church in 1927, the year he also became a British subject. This is reflected in The Journey of the Magi (1927) and Ash Wednesday (1930), poems of quiet and hopeful searching for a higher truth. His play Murder in the Cathedral (1935), based on the martyrdom of Thomas Becket, and Four Quartets (1943) — four related poems written during World War II — represent the culmination of this phase and are perhaps his most personal works.

In 1948 he received the Nobel Prize and the British Order of Merit. His wife Vivienne had died the previous year (he had separated from her in 1933, and in 1937 she had been committed to a mental institution). After the war Eliot was mostly occupied with drama, combining religious themes with contemporary subjects in plays like The Cocktail Party (1949), The Confidential Clerk (1954) and The Elder Statesman (1959). He married again in 1957 and died in London in 1965.

docs.title: Eliot's Modernism
docs.text: Eliot and Ezra Pound were the first great modernist poets of the English language. To the "waste land" of the modern world, Eliot opposed Western culture — the Middle Ages, the Renaissance, Indian philosophy, French Symbolism, modern music including jazz, and the Christian religion. His poetry is difficult, and he himself believed that in our time art should be difficult. The poems of the nihilistic phase are difficult because he removes connective passages and puts disparate images side by side. The poems of the Christian phase are more traditional in structure, but their philosophical background is complex.

docs.title: Poetical Sources
docs.text:
From the Imagists (Ezra Pound) Eliot learned the necessity for clear, precise language and economy of words. From the French Symbolists (especially Baudelaire) he took free verse and the idea that the ugliness of modern city life could be a subject for poetry. He was deeply influenced by John Donne and the Metaphysical poets, admiring their wit and daring images. Perhaps the single greatest influence was Dante: Eliot found in the Divina Commedia the expression not of an individual but of a whole civilisation, and a complete range of emotion from hell's despair to heaven's beatitude.

Two important critical concepts come from Eliot's essays. The first is the "objective correlative": the idea that a poet should not express emotions directly, but should find "a set of objects, a situation, a chain of events" that will produce the same emotion in the reader — so that "when the external facts are given, the emotion is immediately evoked." The second is the "dissociation of sensibility": according to Eliot, modern poetry had lost the unity of intellect and emotion that the Metaphysical poets still had — they were able to "feel their thought as immediately as the odour of a rose."

docs.title: Literary and Social Criticism
docs.text: Eliot was also a very important literary critic. In Tradition and the Individual Talent (1919) he wrote that a writer should have "a perception, not only of the pastness of the past, but of its presence" — the whole of European literature from Homer onwards has a simultaneous existence. In For Lancelot Andrewes (1928) he described himself as "classicist in literature, royalist in politics, and Anglo-Catholic in religion." His social essays, like The Idea of a Christian Society (1939), raise fundamental questions about modern society.

docs.title: The Waste Land (1922)
docs.text: The Waste Land is perhaps the central work in modernist literature. The poem is divided into five sections: "The Burial of the Dead" (lines 1–76), "A Game of Chess" (lines 77–172), "The Fire Sermon" (lines 173–311), "Death by Water" (lines 312–21), and "What the Thunder Said" (lines 322–433).

docs.title: Cultural and Spiritual Sterility
docs.text: The poem is considered by many the most important poem of the 20th century. It expresses the modern artist's disillusion with the world and the desperate need for a new tradition. Eliot sees only ruin and desolation, and is concerned with the decay of Western civilisation after World War I. The central metaphor is the spiritual dryness and sterility of modern life — the death of Western culture through a lack of any belief, religious or other, that can give meaning to everyday existence. The poem cannot be reconstructed into a logical narrative: images are juxtaposed, not ordered. This fragmentation reflects the fragmentation of contemporary culture.

docs.title: The Mythical Structure
docs.text: The Waste Land brings together images of modern decadence with echoes and quotations from ancient myths. Eliot referred his readers to Sir James Frazer's The Golden Bough (1890), a study of pagan myths and fertility rites, and to Jessie L. Weston's From Ritual to Romance (1920), which relates ancient fertility myths to the medieval romances of the Holy Grail. In these romances we see a wasteland whose ruler, the Fisher King, has brought sterility to the land. The order of myth is projected on to the chaos of modern life.

docs.title: Eliot's Method
docs.text: The five unequal sections show no logical continuity. Eliot uses quotations from thirty-five writers in six languages, as well as allusions to philosophical and religious traditions. For example, the crowds flowing over London Bridge at the end of "The Burial of the Dead" are modelled on the crowds of damned souls in Dante's Inferno. The metrical pattern varies: lines change in length and rhythm in a form of free verse, although there are some regularities and rhymes.

docs.title: Checklist
docs.text:
• Eliot: life, American roots, education, career
  • Nihilistic phase: early poetry, nervous breakdown, The Waste Land
  • Christian phase: conversion, Murder in the Cathedral, Four Quartets
  • Eliot's Modernism: difficulty, poetical sources, critical concepts
  • Objective correlative and dissociation of sensibility
  • The Waste Land: structure, themes, mythical method, style

docs.title: Collegamenti
docs.text:
• Italiano: la crisi dei valori e la sterilità del mondo moderno richiamano il pessimismo di Montale (Ossi di seppia) e il suo "correlativo oggettivo", concetto mutuato proprio da Eliot
  • Storia: la Prima guerra mondiale e il crollo dei valori europei, la crisi del 1929, il secondo dopoguerra
  • Filosofia: Bergson e il tempo interiore, F.H. Bradley e la natura psicologica dell'esperienza, la filosofia indiana e il Buddhismo
  • Latino: il rapporto con Dante e la tradizione classica — Eliot vede nella Divina Commedia l'espressione non di un individuo ma di un'intera civiltà
  • Arte: la tecnica del collage nelle avanguardie (Cubismo, Dadaismo) e il metodo del frammento in The Waste Land
  • Scienze: la psicoanalisi di Freud e il flusso di coscienza, il rapporto tra malattia mentale e creatività (il crollo nervoso di Eliot nel 1921)

docs.title: Domande di Esercitazione — Joyce, Eliot, Modernismo
docs.text: Domande di esercitazione per la verifica di martedì. Sono divise per argomento, dalle più brevi (definizioni, fatti) alle più aperte (analisi, confronto).

docs.title: Modernismo in generale
docs.text:
1. Cosa significa il termine "Modernismo"?
  2. Perché all'inizio del Novecento le persone trovavano difficile credere in qualunque cosa?
  3. In che modo la teoria della relatività di Einstein ha influenzato gli scrittori modernisti?
  4. Chi era Nietzsche e come hanno influito le sue idee sulla letteratura inglese?
  5. Cos'è la psicoanalisi e perché Freud è stato così importante per i modernisti?
  6. Elenca almeno quattro caratteristiche principali della letteratura modernista.
  7. Qual è la differenza tra modernisti di prima e di seconda generazione?
  8. Quali tre capolavori sono usciti tra il 1922 e il 1925?
  9. Come vedevano il passato i modernisti? Erano come i Cubisti o i Futuristi?
  10. Chi erano gli "Oxford poets" degli anni Trenta?
  11. Perché alcuni modernisti di seconda generazione andarono a combattere nella Guerra civile spagnola?

docs.title: Poesia moderna
docs.text:
1. Perché gli anni Dieci sono stati un decennio cruciale per la poesia inglese?
  2. Cos'è l'Imagismo? Chi lo ha fondato?
  3. Cos'è il Vorticismo? Su cosa si concentra?
  4. Chi è la figura più importante del Celtic Revival? Cosa commemora Easter 1916?
  5. Perché The Waste Land è considerata la poesia modernista per eccellenza?
  6. Elenca le caratteristiche tipicamente moderniste di The Waste Land.
  7. Chi era W.H. Auden e di cosa parla Refugee Blues?
  8. Chi erano i New Apocalypse poets e perché Dylan Thomas è chiamato "new Romantic"?

docs.title: Romanzo moderno
docs.text:
1. Chi sono i "transitional novelists"? Cita due autori.
  2. Dov'è ambientato Heart of Darkness di Conrad? Cosa mette in discussione il romanzo?
  3. Di cosa parla Sons and Lovers? Perché è importante?
  4. Qual è il tema centrale di Dubliners di Joyce?
  5. Perché Ulysses è considerato un grande romanzo modernista?
  6. Cosa intende Virginia Woolf quando contrappone "time on the clock" e "time in the mind"?
  7. Su cosa è centrato To the Lighthouse?
  8. Cos'è il Bloomsbury Group?
  9. Cosa condivideva E.M. Forster con i modernisti e cosa invece non condivideva?
  10. Cosa esplora A Passage to India?
  11. Che tipo di romanzo è Nineteen Eighty-Four? Di cosa è diventato simbolo Big Brother?

docs.title: Joyce — Vita e contesto
docs.text:
1. Perché Joyce è considerato diverso dagli altri modernisti come Eliot, Woolf e Pound?
  2. Qual era il rapporto di Joyce con l'Irlanda e con la Chiesa cattolica?
  3. Perché Joyce scelse di vivere in esilio e perché tutte le sue opere sono ambientate a Dublino?
  4. Chi era Nora Barnacle e perché la data 16 giugno 1904 è importante?
  5. Perché le opere di Joyce hanno avuto una pubblicazione difficile?
  6. Qual è il legame tra Joyce e Italo Svevo?

docs.title: Dubliners ed Eveline
docs.text:
1. Cosa significa "paralisi" in Dubliners e perché è il tema centrale?
  2. Come è organizzato Dubliners? Cita i quattro stadi.
  3. Cos'è un'epifania nell'opera di Joyce? Sono momenti felici?
  4. Perché Dubliners non è completamente modernista? Spiega il mix tra Realismo e Simbolismo.
  5. In cosa lo stile narrativo di Joyce è diverso da quello di Poe o Henry James?
  6. Riassumi la storia di Eveline. Chi è Frank?
  7. Quale promessa fece Eveline alla madre morente e come la condiziona?
  8. Spiega la scena finale al porto. Perché Eveline non sale sulla nave?

docs.title: The Dead
docs.text:
1. Perché The Dead è il punto culminante di Dubliners?
  2. Chi è Gabriel Conroy? Descrivi la sua personalità.
  3. Chi è Michael Furey e perché è importante per la storia?
  4. Quale canzone sente Gretta alla festa e quale ricordo le risveglia?
  5. Spiega l'epifania di Gabriel alla fine della storia.
  6. Cosa simboleggia la neve nella scena finale?
  7. Perché il finale di The Dead è considerato uno dei più famosi della letteratura inglese?

docs.title: Ulysses
docs.text:
1. Perché Joyce scelse il 16 giugno 1904 come giorno in cui ambientare il romanzo?
  2. Chi sono Leopold Bloom, Stephen Dedalus e Molly Bloom? A quale personaggio omerico corrispondono?
  3. Cos'è il metodo mitico e perché Joyce usa l'Odissea di Omero come modello?
  4. Cos'è il flusso di coscienza? Chi ha coniato il termine?
  5. Elenca le caratteristiche principali del flusso di coscienza di Joyce.
  6. Perché il monologo di Molly è scritto senza punteggiatura?
  7. Cosa significa il "yes" finale del monologo di Molly?
  8. Perché Ulysses ha avuto problemi con la censura?

docs.title: Eliot — Vita e modernismo
docs.text:
1. Dove è nato Eliot e dove ha trascorso la maggior parte della sua vita?
  2. Quali sono le due fasi della poesia di Eliot? Cita un'opera per ogni fase.
  3. Cosa successe a Eliot nel 1921 e come si lega a The Waste Land?
  4. Perché Eliot crede che l'arte moderna debba essere difficile?
  5. Elenca le principali fonti poetiche di Eliot.
  6. Perché Dante è così importante per Eliot?
  7. Spiega il concetto di "correlativo oggettivo".
  8. Spiega il concetto di "dissociation of sensibility" (dissociazione della sensibilità).
  9. Cosa intendeva Eliot quando scrisse che lo scrittore deve avere "una percezione non solo del passato del passato, ma della sua presenza"?

docs.title: The Waste Land
docs.text:
1. Quando è stato pubblicato The Waste Land e com'è strutturato?
  2. Qual è la metafora centrale del poema?
  3. Perché il poema è considerato un ritratto dell'Europa del dopoguerra?
  4. Qual è il ruolo de The Golden Bough di Frazer e di From Ritual to Romance di Jessie L. Weston?
  5. Chi è il Re Pescatore (Fisher King) e come si collega al poema?
  6. Cos'è la tecnica del collage in The Waste Land?
  7. Quante lingue e quanti scrittori sono citati in The Waste Land?

docs.title: The Fire Sermon
docs.text:
1. Da dove proviene il titolo "The Fire Sermon"?
  2. Qual è il tema centrale di questa sezione?
  3. Chi è Mr Eugenides e cosa simboleggiano i ribes (currants) nella sua tasca?
  4. Chi è Tiresia e perché è l'osservatore perfetto per Eliot?
  5. Descrivi la scena della dattilografa e dell'impiegato. Quali sentimenti suscita nel lettore?
  6. Perché la scena della dattilografa è un buon esempio di correlativo oggettivo?
  7. Come usa Eliot il metodo mitico in questa sezione?

docs.title: Confronti e domande aperte
docs.text:
1. Confronta l'uso del mito in Joyce e in Eliot (Ulysses contro The Waste Land).
  2. Sia Joyce che Eliot usano tecniche per frammentare la realtà. In cosa sono simili e in cosa diversi?
  3. Qual è il ruolo della città in Joyce (Dublino) e in Eliot (Londra)?
  4. Entrambi gli autori descrivono un senso di paralisi o sterilità nella vita moderna. Confronta queste due idee.
  5. Perché Joyce ed Eliot sono considerati i due più grandi autori del modernismo inglese?
  6. Scegli un brano studiato (Eveline, The Dead, il monologo di Molly, The Fire Sermon) e spiega perché rappresenta il modernismo.

docs.title: Definizioni rapide da memorizzare
docs.text:
Da saper definire in una o due frasi:

  • Modernismo
  • Paralisi (in Dubliners)
  • Epifania
  • Flusso di coscienza (stream of consciousness)
  • Metodo mitico
  • Correlativo oggettivo
  • Dissociation of sensibility
  • Verso libero (free verse)
  • Imagismo
  • Simbolismo
  • Time on the clock vs time in the mind
  • Monologo interiore

docs.title: Life and Works
docs.text:
Virginia Woolf perfectly represents, as a woman and a writer, the transition between the Victorian and the Modern Age. She was born in London in 1882, the third child of Sir Leslie Stephen, a distinguished Victorian literary critic, and Julia Prinsep Jackson. Her sister Vanessa became an important modernist painter. Virginia grew up in a household full of people devoted to intellectual activities of all kinds — in her own words, "born of well-to-do parents, born into a very communicative, literate, letter writing, visiting, articulate, late nineteenth century world." However, as was the rule for girls of her social class, while her brothers went to university, Virginia was mostly educated at home. She was fully aware that a higher education for women was not what society expected: "A girl had no chance against its fangs. No other desires — say to paint, or to write — could be taken seriously."

Her childhood began happily at her parents' big house at St Ives, Cornwall, but it came to an abrupt end in 1895 when her mother died, followed by a long period of depression. When her half-sister Stella died in 1897, things got worse. These were the first signs of the nervous fragility that accompanied Virginia throughout her life, resulting in periodical fits of mental instability. Her father's death in 1904 produced another period of deep depression and her first attempt at suicide.

After her father's death, the Stephens moved from Kensington to Bloomsbury, where their house became a centre for literary, artistic and philosophical meetings. This group of writers and artists became known as the Bloomsbury Group — they were anti-Victorian, unconventional in their ideas about life and art, and sceptical about religion. Among them were the philosopher Clive Bell, the novelist E.M. Forster, the economist John Maynard Keynes, and Leonard Woolf, a novelist and journalist whom Virginia married in 1912. In 1917 the couple founded the Hogarth Press, which became a major force in English publishing, printing not only Virginia's works but also T.S. Eliot's poems and the English translations of Freud.

Woolf's progress as a novelist reflects her development from realistic to Modernist fiction. Her first two novels, The Voyage Out (1915) and Night and Day (1919), were still fairly conventional. Her mature work began with Jacob's Room (1922), written using the stream-of-consciousness technique. Her subsequent novels — Mrs Dalloway (1925), To the Lighthouse (1927), Orlando (1928) and The Waves (1931) — all followed this model, based on the idea that a novel could not make sense of life simply through realistic description and a conventional plot. Like Joyce, Woolf preferred short meaningful time units: one day in Mrs Dalloway, a few hours in Between the Acts. These short units are expanded beyond their limits by what goes on within the characters' minds, which can cover years and range from past to present to future.

Throughout her life, Woolf was also deeply interested in the Woman Question. As early as 1910 she was working as a volunteer in the women's suffrage movement, and her most famous essay, A Room of One's Own (1929), became a milestone of modern feminism.

In her later years, Virginia became more subject to crises caused by anxiety. When World War II began, the Woolfs moved to Sussex, but the situation did not improve. Unable to face the terror and destruction around her, she drowned herself in the River Ouse on 28 March 1941. Psychiatrists today believe she was affected by bipolar disorder.

docs.title: A Room of One's Own (1929)
docs.text:
In October 1928, Virginia Woolf gave two lectures at Newnham College and Girton College, two women's colleges at the University of Cambridge. These lectures, delivered to audiences of mostly women on the subject of women and literature, were later published in essay form as A Room of One's Own.

The title comes from Woolf's most celebrated image of what women lacked in order to be as good as men at writing: simply a room, time, and a decent income — the freedom to write without having to bother about managing a household all of the time. Woolf's arguments are clearly presented: in the past, women had no existence except in the fiction written by men. Even if a woman had the same gifts as a man, social disadvantages would prevent her from writing or performing any other important activity. Woolf stresses the need for women to gain economic independence and intellectual freedom.

Woolf's rhetorical strategy is built on irony. She opens with a series of provocative statements: women are ignorant, they never made any great scientific or geographical discovery, they never had any ambitions of war and colonialism, they never wrote anything like Shakespeare's plays. What is there except for that? The answer is ironic and polemical: women have been busy giving birth to, taking care of, and nursing the millions of people now inhabiting the world. Without women, no historical or personal enterprise would have been possible.

Woolf's central metaphor is simple and effective: women could not write or create works of art as long as they were too busy working as wives, mothers or sisters, and as long as they did not have a room of their own — a place into which nobody could intrude. She stresses the necessity for women to gain economic independence and go on fighting for their freedom. Only then, maybe in a hundred years, Shakespeare's hypothetical sister, who was as gifted as her brother, will be able to be born again and write great poetry.

The importance of this essay has grown enormously since its publication, and it is now considered a milestone of modern feminism and women's studies.

docs.title: Shakespeare's Sister Will Be Born Some Day
docs.text:
This is a passage from A Room of One's Own in which Woolf directly addresses the young women in her audience. She begins with a list of facts: "You have never made a discovery of any sort of importance. You have never shaken an empire or led an army into battle. The plays of Shakespeare are not by you." These provocative words are not meant as an insult — they are Woolf's ironic way of pointing to the truth: women have never had the opportunity to do these things.

She then reminds her audience of how recent women's rights actually are. It was only in 1866 that the first two colleges for women were created in England. Only since 1880 was a married woman allowed by law to own her own property. And only since 1919 — "which is a whole nine years ago" — was she given a vote. At the time of writing, only about two thousand women were capable of earning over five hundred pounds a year. Given these facts, Woolf argues, we must agree that the lack of opportunity, training, encouragement, and money explains everything.

Then comes the most powerful moment. Woolf recalls Shakespeare's sister — the fictional character she had invented earlier in the essay. This imaginary sister was as gifted as her brother, but she never wrote a word. She died young and "lies buried where the omnibuses now stop, opposite the Elephant and Castle." She was never given the chance to use her talent.

But Woolf's message is not one of despair. She tells her audience that this poet who never wrote a word still lives: "She lives in you and in me, and in many other women who are not here tonight." If women work for her — if they gain economic independence, have a room of their own, and go on fighting for their freedom — then the opportunity will come, and "the dead poet who was Shakespeare's sister will put on the body which she has so often laid down." She will be born again, drawing her life from all the unknown women who came before her.

Woolf's final message is clear: women must prepare the way, so that one day a female Shakespeare can finally exist.
